data.title: Apple Q1 Reinforces My Sell Thesis, But With One Massive Caveat
data.description:
In my two initial articles on Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL), I outlined some of my bearish views on the stock. I've always known this would be a contrarian view for the most part, and shareholders are more aggressive than ever about creating a floor for the stock.

Looking at the company's underwhelming Q1 report, though, I think investors are increasingly questioning the validity of the Apple bull case. Anecdotally, there are far fewer Buy calls now than in the last three years.

My take is that it won't be more than a few more quarters of underwhelming performance to tilt SA Analysts toward a Sell average. I see far more Sells and Strong Sells now than I've seen since last year.

How Did Apple Fare in Q1 2025?

Keeping in mind that Q1 is Apple's most important quarter that straddles the holiday season, the numbers weren't exactly impressive. A 4% YoY quarterly growth rate may well represent their "best quarter ever" with $124.3 billion in revenues, and their "best-ever lineup of products and services" is likely to have contributed to that, but the market was clearly disappointed with the results. AAPL saw a run-up heading into the earnings call, but that didn't last, and the stock gave up most of those gains, leaving the YTD price decline more or less intact.

Apple has rarely ever had issues with bottom-line profitability, so it's not surprising that EPS grew 10% to land at "a record $2.40". That's a great number considering it puts AAPL at a price to forward GAAP earnings multiple of just 32x. It's actually reasonably close to the median figure of 30x, making AAPL's current share price of ~$242 appear to be a bargain.

But hang on, let's keep in mind that this is a Sell thesis, not a Buy call. While these numbers may have impressed some shareholders, the reality of it might not be what you expected. To showcase that, let's look at why Q1 numbers are invariably better at the operating and net profit levels.

OpEx Control is Critical in Terms of Demonstrably Consistent Q1 Profitability

My position is that there's quite a bit of financial engineering needed to deliver strong Q1 results year after year. I think that's what investors are mistaking for long-term stability. Let me show you how this works at the operating level.

If you'll notice, Apple keeps its December quarter (the all-important Q1) operating expenses at their year's lowest, and it's no coincidence that this has been going on for several years. Both SG&A and R&D expenses are at their lowest when the iPhone has its biggest sales quarter. Every year. I find it to be counterintuitive that the strongest quarter invariably sees operating costs drop to their annual troughs.

To me, that's not a sign of healthy growth. My opinion is that Apple is doing whatever it takes to show that its profit levels are pristine when Q1 comes rolling around. It doesn't make sense for operating expenses to exhibit such an odd seasonal pattern, but a novice investor is not likely to be aware of that. All they can see is that Apple is growing its EBIT margins in a healthy way. Based on the graph above, I'd have to disagree.

There's another interesting takeaway here: Revenue slowdowns make it harder to post strong YoY operating margins in subsequent Q1s.

What do I mean by this?

Let's look at YoY quarterly revenue growth. In Q1 2023 (December 2022 quarter), the top line declined by nearly 5.5%, and yet, operating margin grew to 30.74% of revenue. The biggest reason (other than a gradual expansion at the gross margin level) is that R&D was cut to 6.6% of revenue and SG&A was cut to 5.6%. I've reproduced the same graph from above, but with line item labels for those two expenditures.

If you move forward four quarters to Q1 2024 (December 2023 quarter), you'll see that Apple needed to bring its total OpEx down to 12.11% in order to allow the operating margin to grow significantly between Q1 2023 and Q1 2024, from 30.74% to 33.76%. This happened as revenue grew just 2.1% on a YoY basis in Q1 2024. This isn't operating leverage at work; it's pure financial engineering. Perfectly above board and legitimate, but nonetheless confusing to anyone who doesn't look at the fine print.

Further ahead in the timeline, if we move forward again to Q1 2025, which is the last reported quarter, we see both SG&A and R&D down again, but to a lesser degree because top-line growth came in at 4% this time. This allowed Apple to avoid deep cuts to those two line items, but still show a 70bps increase in the operating margin. When revenue growth is strong, Apple doesn't need to micromanage its operating costs all that much because investor attention is now on the top line rather than the operating margin. They're still depressed compared to other quarters, but less so.

I'm not saying it's wrong for Apple to engage in this kind of financial engineering, but it doesn't allow shareholders to easily spot the weaknesses in the company's growth story. We already know iPhone unit sales are no longer reported, but even the weak features that Cook found it worth highlighting were definitely a disappointment:

the A18-powered devices have advanced features like extended battery life and enhanced camera capabilities, resonating with customers globally.

Seriously? Battery life and a better camera are now iPhone's most important selling points?

And now that we're aware of these operational adjustments to show growth in Q1, I have less confidence than ever about Apple being an innovator anymore.

Another upside consideration gone south: There's been a lot of talk about Apple Intelligence, but all it is, is an integration of ChatGPT and other generative models with existing features - Siri, searching photos using natural language, Messages and Mail summaries, and a few others that aren't really trailblazing features.

It shouldn't come as a surprise to any of us that Apple needs to resort to financial engineering to keep its shareholders happy. Within that context, the outlook for Q2 2025 looks impressive but is actually not. When you consider the fact that Q2 2024 showed a 4.3% revenue decline, which is the worst since Q1 2023, you suddenly realize that even if revenues were to "grow in the low- to mid-single digits year-over-year, despite a projected 2.5% foreign exchange headwind", it would only be a minor recovery of sorts and not necessarily the kind of strong growth that propels the investment narrative forward.

The Share Repurchase Angle

Apple regularly boasts its returns to shareholders via share buybacks, but the yield on those has been deteriorating over the past two years as the stock made significant gains. Moreover, the company stepped up its buyback cadence in the last two years, which makes even less sense when you look at price appreciation.

Buybacks can be a very effective tool for shareholder returns, but only when they're used judiciously and to achieve better yields over time - not worse. And yet, Apple last year announced its biggest buyback program ever, worth $110 billion - with a deal-sweetening 4% increase in the dividend.

What's clear to me is that Apple needs to use every means possible to ensure a healthy total return for its investors. In the absence of strong topline growth, the only way to do that is with aggressive buybacks and dividend yields. The only problem with that is, it hasn't been working over the past six months.

True, longer timeframes do show outperformance, but I think the glory days are now behind Apple, and going by how analysts on SA finally seem to be arriving at that consensus, I reiterate my Sell rating.

My view is that Apple is undeniably at a very mature stage in its business cycle. Without any significant innovations on the horizon (and what happened to Vision Pro, by the way? It was mentioned a grand total of 3 times on the Q1 earnings call!), the stock is going to trudge along, trying to keep up with the market by displaying its superior financial acumen Q1 after Q1. How long can that last before investors start to get anxious about their capital being stuck in a rut?

Possible Solutions to Your AAPL Problem

Long-term investors are in a particularly unique predicament because they now face long-term capital gains tax if they sell off their AAPL holding, but the reality is that they may not need to. That's what my massive caveat at the end of the article is all about.

For all others who bought Apple relatively late and whose cost basis is not too far away from the current share price, my recommendation is to Sell, then render unto Caesar what is Caesar's, and finally rotate your after-tax proceeds into a better investment.

There's also another alternative. Let's say you don't want to sell your shares outright because your cost basis is too close to or above the current share price. In that situation (or if you want to bring your cost basis further down, and you're also willing to sell at a slight premium), you can sell near-the-money covered calls.

In this example, underwriting one contract expiring at the end of February will get you a $280 premium up front on a $245 strike. If AAPL hits that level before or at contract expiry, your shares get called away at that strike price, and your total profit would be the difference between $245 and your cost basis per share, plus the premium of $2.80 per share. If your shares aren't called away, you can just roll that contract over and over, gradually bringing your cost basis down over time.

I have no doubt that AAPL has been a generational opportunity for the past several decades, but it no longer represents that. Maybe it's time to move on, but please check with your tax advisor before you make any move.

My Massive Caveat and Concluding Thoughts

I'll leave you with one major caveat here that I think long-term Apple permabulls can really get behind. If you bought the bulk of your AAPL stocks BEFORE 2010, DO NOT SELL YOUR SHARES! I want to make it amply clear that long-term investors don't need to do anything but keep holding the stock because your annual dividend yield is likely to be in the double or triple digit percentages by now.

It would be decidedly imprudent to sell an asset that's giving you that kind of yield year after year. This would then become a willable asset that your descendants can live very comfortably on.

For anyone else, I would recommend a Sell or a perpetually-rolling-over covered call options play like the one I outlined above. The sooner you can break even on your AAPL investment, the better, because that yield on cost will keep becoming juicier the more you bring down your cost basis with this strategy. If you don't want to risk your shares being called away, just go further out the money with your strike. The premiums are obviously lower, and it will take you longer to reach breakeven on your cost basis, but you get to retain your shares until they eventually start paying for themselves - over and over and year after year as long as Apple's dividends are kept intact and growing.

Although my rating is a Sell, I'm providing these alternative scenarios and strategies for different categories of Apple investors. It's a great company, no doubt, but unless you're getting a return that's superior to any of your other investments (or you create that path via options), it's not worth holding on to the stock, let alone considering a buy.

data.title: SMCY: Collecting Income From Super Micro Computer Uncertainty
data.description:
Overview

The folks over at YieldMax continue to release new funds that track different companies. One of the newer funds is YieldMax SMCI Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:SMCY), which aims to track the underlying price movements of Super Micro Computer (SMCI). SMCY is one of the newest funds and launched as recently as September 2024. Therefore, we have a smaller window of operating performance, and assets under management only total approximately $112M. Additionally, the fund's performance has been poor since its launch, which can scare investors away from considering this high-yielding fund.

SMCY currently sports an estimated annual dividend yield greater than 97%, according to the fund's overview. However, readers should note that the yield changes every month as distributions are announced. Since SMCY's inception, the price has already deteriorated by more than 42% in less than 6 months. Even with the massive dividend yield, the total return still sits around negative 20%. So why would investors even consider a fund like SMCY? Well, income investors may be able to capitalize on the underlying uncertainty around SMCI's success, price volatility, and outlook. SMCI is in a highly competitive space and is constantly lumped against notable peers such as NVIDIA (NVDA).

Super Micro Computer's price has been in a near-constant decline over the last twelve months. This can be attributed to the fact that SMCY has experienced a similar price decline. The key to making SMCY a successful investment would be to time your entry in anticipation of when SMCI's price stabilizes. If SMCI's price decline eventually stops and stabilizes, SMCY will be a much more attractive investment. The high level of income may even allow investors to outperform common shareholders of SMCI if the price continues to have choppy trading sessions over the short term. So let's first start by taking a look at the fund's strategy and approach to generating income.

Fund Strategy & Risks

SMCY operates a synthetic option strategy to generate the necessary income that it needs to fund the high distribution rate. The term 'synthetic' refers to the fact that SMCY doesn't actually hold any of the underlying common shares of Super Micro Computer. This can elevate the risk profile and cause SMCY to trade with a greater amount of volatility. SMCY essentially mimics the daily price changes of SMCI by simultaneously buying SMCI call options and selling SMCI put options. The most recent prospectus lays out the different option strategies implemented pretty clearly.

I like the fact that SMCY makes use of different OTM (out-of-the-money) option strategies because it allows for a greater amount of price appreciation to be captured. For simplicity's sake, let's say that SMCI's price currently trades at $40 per share. SMCY may write an option against SMCI and select a strike price of $45 per share. If SMCI has a strong earnings report and the price spikes up to $50 per share, SMCY would capture the growth up to the strike price of $45 per share. Even though the gains above the strike price would essentially be sacrificed, SMCY would still capture a meaningful amount of upside.

The downside that we've been experiencing is that SMCY partakes in the full weight of downside movements. So unlike the capped upside, the downward risks are much more present. If SMCI's price continues to decline back to prior lows, SMCY could continue to reach new lows. Since SMCY's inception, the share price of Super Micro Computer has fallen by about 7%. Over the same period, SMCY's price has declined by nearly 45% since it has struggled to recover from the initial drop in late October, caused by several headwinds. SMCI's price was on the decline due to issues such as a delayed 10-Q filing, mixed reactions to an announced stock split, and its prior Q1 earnings.

This reveals the first downside to YieldMax funds: price decay. The limited upside potential caused by the option strategies makes it very difficult to see a price recovery following a rapid price decline. SMCY isn't the only fund to experience such an issue, but it has the unique disadvantage of these issues happening right after SMCY's initial launch. Just as a reference, we can see that there are plenty of other YieldMax funds that seem to be stuck in the downward price trend. Obviously, each fund aims to track a different underlying company, but I want this to demonstrate the issue and potential risk that a position in SMCY would entail.

While there are some YieldMax funds that have appreciated in value over time, those funds are definitely part of the minority. For reference, some of the funds that have increased in share price are the following:

  • YieldMax NVDA Option Income Strategy ETF (NVDY)
  • YieldMax MSTR Option Income Strategy ETF (MSTY)
  • YieldMax META Option Income Strategy ETF (FBY)

The high income received from SMCY would definitely come at a price if the price doesn't eventually stabilize, and there is a lot of uncertainty surrounding SMCI's future that investors must take into consideration. I wouldn't recommend SMCY for investors that have a high value of capital preservation. Lastly, SMCY's assets under management only amount to a little over $112M. There is a risk of liquidation if the price continues to decline and investors pull their capital out of the fund.

Super Micro Computer Uncertainty

SMCI's price has now declined by nearly 45% over the last twelve months. The stock faced several internal and external headwinds that caused the price to continuously slide. However, I believe that the underlying fundamentals are strong, which makes SMCI a solid long-term play. In the short term, SMCY shareholders can benefit from the elevated volatility. Some of the issues that have contributed to the price decline consist of the following:

  1. SMCI was investigated by the DOJ.
  2. No evidence of fraud was found.
  3. Q1 earnings were lower than Wall St. hoped for and shares declined.
  4. Delayed filing of its Form 10-K.
  5. SMCI discloses Nasdaq non-compliance letter.
  6. Removed from the Nasdaq-100 Index and replaced by Palantir (PLTR).
  7. Negatively impacted price movement from DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK).

While I am not debating whether or not the validity of these issues is a good reason to sell, I do understand why it has investors spooked. It makes sense why investors would be hesitant to invest their hard-earned money into a company that has their auditor, EY, resign from them. EY stated that it was unwilling to be a part of the financial reporting that SMCI provided.

However, I still see these things as short-term noise that doesn't change the underlying financial strength that the company has demonstrated. The company earned an A- grade, driven by its massive revenue, EPS, and EBITDA year-over-year growth. The latest earnings update indicates that EPS came in between $0.58 and $0.60 per share and revenue increased 54% year over year, falling within a range of $5.6B to $5.7B. The business ended the quarter with $1.4B in cash on hand, putting SMCI in a great position to maneuver headwinds and invest in new opportunities for growth.

With these results in mind, the company still trades at an extremely attractive valuation. SMCI also earns an A- grade on valuation due to its current forward price-to-earnings ratio of 16.69x, which significantly undercuts the sector median price-to-earnings ratio of 29.72x. Additionally, SMCI trades at a price-to-book ratio of 3.52x, which also undercuts the sector median of 4.46x.

Forward-looking, SMCI ramped up their production of NVIDIA's Blackwell, which can drive additional growth once these efforts materialize. These are systems that are efficient scaling of AI workloads, which is aided by improved cooling capacities. SMCI continues to align itself to benefit from the rising demand for different AI innovations. The size of the AI market is estimated to grow at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 27.67% through 2030. SMCI can continue to leverage its partnership with NVIDIA to capture a greater amount of market share year over year.

Dividend Utilization

SMCI doesn't offer investors a dividend at the moment, so SMCY makes it possible to collect income where it usually isn't. As of the latest declared monthly dividend of $1.7215 per share, the current dividend yield sits as high as 97% at the moment. However, we can already see that the distribution amounts have been on the decline, following the same trajectory as the underlying price movement. Although the payouts have been inconsistent recently, we can see increases over time, assuming that SMCI's price stabilizes and starts to move in some positive direction.

Additionally, the amount of the distribution is determined by factors such as the underlying stock's performance, time until expiration, efficiency of the options, and implied volatility levels. The higher the uncertainty, the higher the premiums are. Therefore, there's a chance that we gradually see an uptick in the distribution payouts in the short term. Management confirmed that they would be able to get all reporting finalized by the Feb 25th deadline.

This distribution history is so short, but assuming that it remains within the same range, I believe that the high amount of dividend income collected can be put to good use. I, personally, implement a dividend wheel strategy with all of the dividend income I collect from different YieldMax funds. I collect these dividends and then allocate them to other areas of my portfolio that may offer a better value proposition for a long-term investment.

For instance, I may allocate the dividends towards consumer-facing companies that are impacted by uncertainty around Trump's tariffs or different real estate investments that remain at suppressed valuations because of higher interest rates. This strategy helps me build up my core long-term positions at a much faster rate. In this specific instance, I may even use SMCY distributions to help me accumulate more shares of SMCI as the long-term potential still looks attractive. An alternative approach would be to accumulate all of the distributions received until you reach 'house money' status. This is when all distributions received total more than your initial investment amount. Since SMCY's projected annual yield is around 97%, investors should technically be able to collect over 100% of their initial investment amount back in a little over a year's time.

However, the distributions received will have some tax consequences that investors must take into consideration. The latest Section 19A notice is dated from October, which may no longer be an accurate representation. Using it as an example, we can see that SMCY's distributions were funded by 41% net investment income and nearly 59% of return of capital. Distributions funded by net investment income are typically classified as ordinary dividends, which have the most unfavorable tax consequences.

Return of capital distributions don't typically have immediate tax consequences if they aren't considered income. ROC distributions are used when the fund hasn't earned enough income to actually support the distribution. However, ROC distributions reduce your cost basis, meaning that you are basically deferring your taxes until the time of sale.

Takeaway

In conclusion, SMCY could be an effective way to capture a high level of income from the underlying uncertainty revolving around Super Micro Computer. I have initiated positions in both SMCY and SMCI so that I can benefit from the short-term volatility and long-term potential. Although the price has rapidly deteriorated since inception, SMCY had unfortunate timing with its launch, as SMCI went through a series of headwinds that drove the price lower. Despite these challenges, SMCI has delivered strong financial results, with revenue growth of 54% year over year. Once the market recognizes this strong growth, momentum can shift to a more positive outlook. A more positive outlook would stop SMCY's price bleed and create a more favorable environment that allows better price stability and income generation.

data.title: Why Growth In U.S. Crude Exports Is Synonymous With Growth Of NYMEX WTI
data.description:
By Amanda Townsley

At a Glance

  • Over the past 15 years, Cushing has seen a dramatic increase in its tankage and pipeline connectivity, solidifying its position as the world's largest crude oil hub
  • Crude oil traded across North America is negotiated and transacted as a differential to the WTI futures contract

2025 marks the 10th anniversary of the lifting of the U.S. crude oil export ban, a development that helped launch North America into the top echelon of global oil suppliers while also recasting the landscape for crude oil trading.

Previously, a system designed for importing crude oil, the North American grid flipped outward, upending price dynamics and forming new trading hubs. As the North American crude oil trading system has adapted to its place in the global pricing system, here's a look at what's changed, what hasn't and what might be next.

The push to export crude oil after a 40-year ban resulted from burgeoning production and a race to build the infrastructure to efficiently deliver it to market. From a low of 8 million barrels per day in 2008, Canadian and U.S. oil supply grew to 13 million in 2015, and rose to 18 million in 2024. Over that period, production that had been dwindling began pushing the onshore pipeline system to capacity, requiring major investment to reroute flows and deliver the growing supply to refineries, trading hubs and coastal docks for export.

At the heart of the vast North American crude oil trading system is the NYMEX Light Sweet Crude Oil futures contract, often called "WTI". The price of WTI futures represents the outright price of a barrel of domestic light sweet crude oil at Cushing, Oklahoma. Cushing has been an effective delivery location for crude oil since the 1980s, as a vibrant hub for cash market trading of crude oil with an unparalleled network of pipelines, refineries and storage terminals.

If the Cushing of the 1980s was a "vibrant" hub, the Cushing of the last decade would be akin to a thriving metropolis. As the epicenter of both the pipeline and trading system, Cushing's tankage and pipeline connectivity has ballooned along with the North American grid over the last 15 years. The pipeline system channels crude oil from Western Canada, the Rockies, Bakken, Permian and regional plays into Cushing, where it can be stored, blended or delivered to local refineries and onto the Gulf Coast. In 2024, Cushing was home to 98 million barrels of crude oil storage capacity, making it the largest single hub in the world. Over 20 inbound pipelines, 16 outbound pipelines and more than 30 intra-Cushing lines provide unrivaled connectivity between a diverse mix of operators, across terminals and between production basins and other market hubs like the Gulf Coast.

Despite recent low inventory levels, records are being set at Cushing. In 2024, WTI futures set a record for physical deliveries of over 317K contracts, or 317 million barrels, the highest level since 2017. WTI futures rank number two globally for physical deliveries, behind the GME's Oman futures contract, and in 2024 saw twice the volume exchanged for barrels of the physically delivered Gulf Coast futures contract.

Whether global oil inventories remain near the low end of the historical range or build back toward historical highs, the ample export capacity and responsive pricing system mean that strong correlations between U.S., Cushing, and global inventories will continue.

WTI: So Much More than Crude at Cushing

As Cushing is the heart of the oil pipeline system, the WTI futures contract is the heart of the North American trading system.

Centered around the price of crude oil at Cushing is a long-established trading ecosystem that allows traders to efficiently price oil no matter its location or quality. Crude oil traded anywhere from Hardisty, Alberta, to Corpus Christi, Texas, is negotiated and transacted as a differential, or spread, to the WTI futures contract. The spread at which a specific grade of crude oil trades versus the NYMEX price reflects differences in quality, transportation costs and regional supply-demand variables. Traders carefully measure these factors to properly value different types of crude oil, as well as to route their crude oil to the most profitable destination.

Spot physical crude oil trades actively during the month before it will be delivered. Price reporting agencies such as Argus Media and Platts aggregate transactions for various grades on a daily basis and publish a daily differential index price. Price differentials are typically within a few dollars above or below NYMEX WTI, but certain grades or locations can trade at double-digit discounts. The price a seller will receive for the crude oil will be NYMEX WTI plus the differential at which the trade is executed. Rather than transacting at a fixed differential, producers and consumers can also choose to buy or sell crude at the index price: the monthly average of the differential published by the price reporting agency plus the monthly average of NYMEX WTI.

The Rise of Grades: Hedging a Differential to WTI

The trading ecosystem aggregates the majority of price discovery and liquidity at a single location: WTI futures. This allows the bulk of risk management and hedging to occur nearly around the clock, with low cost and high transparency. This also means that most of the time, fluctuations of the price of crude at Cushing via WTI futures is the main driver of fluctuations in the price of crude oil produced, traded or exported across North America.

Even before the shale boom, the large geographic footprint and quality diversity in North American crude oil gave rise not just to active physical trading in other hubs, but also demand for crude oil hedging instruments beyond domestic light sweet at Cushing.

In 2007, NYMEX launched a series of futures contracts to meet this need, offering traders the ability to hedge the price differentials around the U.S. The initial futures contracts cash-settled to the prices of Mars, West Texas Sour (WTS), and Louisiana Light Sweet (LLS) published by Argus Media, the same differentials that underpinned most physical transactions.

The expansion of the U.S. pipeline network and crude supply in the following decade led to the birth of new grades of crude oil and new trading hubs in North America. As both a refining center and a geographically convenient outlet for exports, the focus on Houston along the Gulf Coast grew. With strong quality parameters and broad connectivity to Permian production and the refining network, Magellan's East Houston terminal (also called "MEH") emerged as the hub. In 2015, Argus began assessing "WTI Houston" - the price differential to NYMEX WTI of WTI Midland-quality crude oil at MEH. The WTI Houston assessment quickly became the standard of value for light sweet crude oil on the Gulf Coast. Any trader that buys or sells crude oil on the Gulf Coast today will look to the Argus WTI Houston differential plus NYMEX WTI as the key indicator for the value of that crude oil. The outright price of crude oil on the Gulf Coast cannot be determined without the price of NYMEX WTI.

A CME Group futures contract for the Argus WTI Houston differential to WTI, known by the industry as its exchange code "HTT", began trading in 2016. With the Argus WTI Houston differential and the NYMEX WTI futures contracts as the underlying prices for physical transactions, HTT was welcomed by the industry as a convenient hedge for the quality and location of the growing grade of crude.

The flexibility to add or subtract new grades and locations as the market shifts is a hallmark and benefit of the U.S. crude trading system. In 2025, open interest in these types of grades futures contracts, priced as differentials to NYMEX WTI, is approaching 800 million barrels and extends out four years. Beyond WTI and Brent, this represents more barrels than any other oil futures contract globally and highlights both CME's HTT futures contract as the instrument of choice for managing risk on the U.S. Gulf Coast, and NYMEX WTI as the central pricing mechanism for crude oil.

What WTI Means for the Rest of the World

The sheer scale of U.S. oil export capability has meant that U.S. crude oil prices have influenced crude oil prices globally for years. The WTI-Brent futures spread reflects the price difference between the U.S. and North Sea crude oil markets. At the start of the shale boom, the spread flipped from a positive value to as low as negative $25 per barrel. By 2020, the build out of U.S. export infrastructure narrowed the spread, with WTI trading $3 to $5 under Brent during 2024.

As a nod to the significance of U.S. crude supply and the decline in European production, in 2023 U.S. crude oil was added to the basket of deliverable grades under the Brent futures contract. According to Argus and Platts, U.S. WTI Midland crude oil - priced off of WTI futures - has since been setting the price of Dated Brent more than 50% of the time. This shift made explicit that anyone with exposure to the Brent complex also has exposure to U.S. NYMEX WTI crude prices, and led to the adoption of NYMEX WTI-based cargo pricing for European refiners.

In the tenth year of North America's development as a global crude oil export powerhouse, some changes are only just beginning. Given the meteoric rise in North American exports, international use of WTI continues to grow. Trading volume of WTI originating outside the U.S. hit a high of 37% during 2024, up from 29% year-on-year, and the majority of new participants in both WTI futures and CME's WTI Houston futures came from outside of the U.S.

The NYMEX WTI Light Sweet crude oil futures contract has been around for 42 years, serving as the benchmark for U.S. oil prices and underpinning the prices of consumer goods like gasoline, diesel and propane - and the price of all types of crude oil around North America and even overseas. With crude oil on the Gulf Coast valued and traded as a differential to WTI futures, growth in the importance of Gulf Coast crude oil is synonymous with the WTI Houston marker and the WTI futures contract that sits at its core. NYMEX WTI has truly gone global.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
